spans.content: Enhanced thermal stability of nanograined metals
X. Zhou, X. Y. Li,* K. Lu*
Enhanced thermal stability of nano-grained metals below a critical grain size
X. Zhou,1,2 X.Y. Li,1 and K. Lu1
1 Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science, Institute of Metal Research,
Chinese Academy of Sciences, 72 Wenhua Road, Shenyang 110016, China
2 School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology
in purity) and pure Ni (99.5 wt.
, respectively, to form fully recrystallized structures. The
rods of Cu with a diameter of
and Ni with a diameter of 9 mm were processed
by using surface mechanical grinding treatment (SMGT) (28) in liquid nitrogen at 77
K with processing parameters listed in Table S1. For each sample, the SMGT process
was repeated several times for achieving a thick and uniform gradient nano-grained
surface layer. The processed surface is very smooth with a roughness of
No surface crack was identified in the as-SMGT samples.
Structural characterization of the SMGT and the annealed samples were carried out on
scanning electron microscopes (SEM, FEI Nova 430 and Verios 460) and a
transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20), respectively. Texture
components in the surface layer of the as-SMGT Cu were determined by using TEM
and EBSD equipped in SEM, respectively. The processed surface was protected by
electrodepositing a thin Cu layer (for Cu samples) and Ni layer (for Ni samples),
respectively. Cross-sectional sample preparation for SEM and TEM observations
follows the ordinary route of cutting, mechanical polishing, and final electro-chemical
polishing or thinning. The final finishing was accomplished by electro-chemical
method using an electrolyte consisting of
for Cu and an electrolyte consisting of
Three types of thermal annealing treatment were performed for characterize the grain
size effect on thermal stability of nano-grained Cu and Ni samples. The annealing
treatments were carried out in a tube furnace with protecting Ar atmosphere and a
a) Step-wise annealing followed by ex-situ SEM observations: The as-SMGT Cu
sample was coated by an electrodeposited Cu thin layer and a cross-sectional sample
was prepared by cutting and polishing ready for SEM observations. The sample was
firstly annealed in Ar atmosphere at
observations of the same area in the cross-sectional sample. After SEM observation,
the sample was reheated up to 393 K, holding for 30 min and cooling down for another
SEM observation. This procedure was repeated for several times to a final annealing
temperature of 523 K, each time with a temperature increment of
b) Direct heating and annealing at preset temperatures: The as-SMGT samples was
to different preset temperatures, holding for 30 min and
cooling down for SEM and TEM observations, respectively. For Cu, the annealing
S1). For Ni, the annealing temperatures are 573, 673, 773, 873, 973, 1073, and 1173 K,
respectively (Fig. S2). Both Fig. S1 and S2 showed that some nano-grains in the surface
layer are stable without coarsening at elevated temperatures while significant
coarsening occurred in the submicro-sized grains.
c) Isothermal annealing: The as-SMGT Cu sample was heated up to
for different durations (0.5, 1, 2, 5, and 12 hours, respectively) for study the effect of
annealing time on grain coarsening (Fig. S3). The nano-grains in the surface layer are
stable during annealing at this temperature for 12 hours.
Nano-hardness tests of samples before and after annealing were performed on a
Hysitron Tribo-indenter. Measurement conditions include: a preset load of
, and a holding time of 2 seconds. Nano-hardness
values were averaged with at least 10 indentations. The spacing between two
The excess energy of grain boundary (GB) in the nano-grained surface layer of the
SMGT Cu samples was determined by using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC)
on a TA DSC at a heating rate of
grain sizes were measured. The top surface layer of
the as-prepared SMGT sample for the DSC measurements. The subsurface layers with
respectively. Each sample was scanned twice for an accurate determination of the
exothermal enthalpy released from grain coarsening. With the measured exothermal
, the GB excess energy (γ) can be estimated by
the change of GB area per unit mass, which is determined by the grain size change
measured from TEM and SEM observations.
The composition analysis was carefully performed by using EDAX and EELS equipped
in a FEI Tecnai F30 TEM and a FEI Tecnai F20 TEM to check the possible
contamination in the top surface layer of SMGT samples. The EDAX line scanning
results showed no obvious carbon or oxygen can be detected in the top surface layer
and no obvious variations along the depth, as shown in Fig. S4. No obvious impurities
were detected in EELS analysis of light elements in the top surface layer as depth
In order to eliminate the effect of possible processing-induced contamination in the
surface layer, we used the same SMGT process but lower rotation speed (20 rpm) and
to prepare a submicro-grained surface layer. Compared
with the process for the nano-grained surface layer, lower strain rates (but comparable
accumulated plastic strain) and higher temperature resulted in larger grain sizes,
in the top surface layer. The level impurity induced by SMGT process
in the top surface layer with submicro-grains is comparable to that with nano-grains.
The submicro-grained sample was annealed at
under SEM. As shown in Fig. 2, grain coarsening appears in the topmost surface layer,
rather than in the subsurface layer as seen in the nano-grained surface layer. This is a
direct evidence showing that the high thermal stability of the top surface layer in the
SMGT sample is a nano-scale grain size effect, rather than a result of processing-

deep, meaning the texture is uniform along depth from surface. The IPF image also
